raw_case:
An uncommon localization of deep benign fibrous histiocytoma in the thigh: A case report
Benign fibrous histiocytoma (BFH) is a rare mesenchymal tumor, with its deep variant (DBFH) accounting for only 1%-2% of cases. DBFH typically arises in the limbs, head, and neck, and usually measures under 12 cm. MRI can suggest the diagnosis by detailing the tumor's location and internal features. However, histopathological examination remains essential for definitive diagnosis, supported by immunohistochemical staining. The treatment is always surgical, involving complete resection of the tumor with clean excision margins.

Introduction

Benign fibrous histiocytoma (BFH) is a soft tissue tumor which is defined histologically as a mesenchymal tumor characterized by the presence of fibroblasts and histiocytes [1]. Two types have been described, the cutaneous form, which is the most common, and the deep form, which is the subject of our paper and constitutes a very rare entity (around 1% to 2% of all HFBs) [2]. Deep benign fibrous histiocytoma (DBHF) affects the limbs, head and neck, and often measures less than 12 cm. We report the case of a 16 cm HFBP discovered in the thigh of a young patient, describing its clinical features, imaging evaluation, histopathological and immunophenotypic characteristics, and management.

Case report

We report the case of a 55-year-old patient with no significant medical history who presented to our facility with a progressively enlarging mass in the thigh that had been evolving over the past 6 months. There were no associated symptoms, such as fever or signs of inflammation, and the patient's general condition remained well preserved. Clinical examination revealed a palpable, nonvisible mass on the anteromedial aspect of the left thigh, which was firm, tissue-like in consistency, nonfluctuant, and nontender (Fig. 1). The remainder of the examination showed no associated lymphadenopathy, pulmonary, or digestive symptoms, and hip mobility was preserved. We performed magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which revealed an iso-intense T1 and hyper-intense T2 lesion that enhanced heterogeneously after contrast injection, measuring 16 cm in its largest dimension. The mass was deeply situated between the sartorius and adductor longus muscles, with surrounding infiltration extending to the level of the neurovascular bundle within the femoral triangle (Figs. 2 and 3).Given this radiological description, we admitted the patient to the operating room. Under spinal anesthesia and in the dorsal decubitus position, we approached the mass through an anteromedial incision of the thigh. Intraoperatively, we discovered a well-circumscribed, encapsulated tissue mass that did not infiltrate the skin or adjacent muscles (Fig. 4). We completely excised the mass with a 1 cm margin of resection and sent it for histopathological examination (Fig. 5).Macroscopically, the tumor was soft, well-circumscribed, and encapsulated, with a red-brown cut surface. Microscopic examination revealed a benign proliferation of spindle-shaped cells with oval, wavy nuclei, lacking cytological atypia and mitoses. The cytoplasm was sparse, and the stroma was rich in collagen bundles. Immunohistochemical analysis showed strong, diffuse positive staining for CD34 in the tumor cells, focal positive staining for Desmin, and negative staining for β-catenin, ruling out a desmoid tumor. Additionally, the tumor was negative for H-caldesmon and AML, excluding smooth muscle tumors; S100 and SOX10, excluding nerve tumors; MDM2 and CDK4, excluding liposarcoma; CD31 and HHV8, excluding vascular tumors; STAl6, excluding solitary fibrous tumors; and Myogenin, excluding striated muscle tumors what was in favor of a deep benign fibrous histiocytoma.The patient was discharged the day after the operation. No additional therapeutic investigations were required, and the 2-year follow-up showed a favorable outcome with no signs of recurrence.

Discussion

Deep benign fibrous histiocytoma (DBFH) unlike the cutaneous type, is a rare form [1]. Diagnosing DBFH relies heavily on histopathological analysis of the removed mass because its clinical and MRI features can closely resemble those of other benign and malignant tumors, such as schwannomas, leiomyosarcomas, solitary fibrous tumors (SFT), dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP), and malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST). When DBFH is detected on MRI, its deep location, size, and enhancement patterns can raise concerns about the possibility of malignancy, making accurate diagnosis challenging [3].Radiologically, conventional radiography reveals a superficial, radiodense soft tissue mass in the proximal medial thigh, displaying no signs of mineralization or femoral involvement, providing nonspecific findings. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) typically demonstrates these tumors with a T2 hyperintense signal, suggesting a composition rich in water or cellular content. Although predominantly homogeneous, the mass may exhibit areas of internal necrosis and hemorrhage accompanied by central vascularity. Benign fibrous histiocytomas usually do not display T2-hyperintense peritumoral edema, which can aid in their differential diagnosis from more aggressive tumors [4].The treatment of choice for deep FBH is complete surgical resection. It is crucial to perform resection with healthy margins of around 1 to 2 cm around the tumor to minimize the risk of local recurrence [5]. The need for chemotherapy or radiotherapy is rare and typically reserved for atypical cases or situations where the tumor is malignant or has shown transformative features. Radiotherapy may be considered for unresectable tumors, or to complement treatment if healthy margins cannot be obtained, but this is the exception rather than the norm [5].Regular follow-up is essential after surgery to monitor any possible recurrence. Follow-up examinations may include periodic clinical consultations and imaging, such as ultrasound or MRI, to assess the absence of recurrence or the presence of new lesions [3].

Conclusion

Deep benign fibrous histiocytoma (DBFH) is a type of soft tissue tumor characterized histologically by fibroblasts and histiocytes. While benign, its deep tissue occurrence complicates diagnosis and treatment, as it can be easily confused with other soft tissue tumors.

Patient consent

Consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 55-year-old patient  
- **Chief Complaint:** A progressively enlarging mass in the left thigh  
- **History of Present Illness:** Mass in the anteromedial thigh gradually increasing in size for 6 months. No associated symptoms such as fever, inflammation, or systemic symptoms. Preserved general condition.  
- **Past Medical History:** None  
- **Personal History:** None  
- **Family History:** None  
- **Physical Examination:** Palpable, firm, nonfluctuant, nontender mass located on the anteromedial aspect of the left thigh. No lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary or digestive symptoms, and normal hip mobility.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **MRI:** Iso-intense T1 and hyper-intense T2 lesion with heterogenous contrast enhancement, measuring 16 cm, located between the sartorius and adductor longus muscles, with infiltration towards the neurovascular bundle in the femoral triangle.  
  - **Histopathology:** Encapsulated tumor showing benign spindle-shaped cells with no cytological atypia or mitoses, sparsely cytoplasmic stroma, and collagen-rich bundles.  
  - **Immunohistochemistry:** Positive for CD34 and Desmin; Negative for markers ruling out desmoid tumor, smooth muscle tumors, nerve tumors, liposarcoma, vascular tumors, solitary fibrous tumor, and striated muscle tumors.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:** Based on the location, size, and heterogenous enhancement on MRI, potential diagnoses included benign and malignant tumors such as schwannomas, leiomyosarcomas, solitary fibrous tumors, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP), and malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST).  
2. **Exclusion of Malignant Tumors:** Histology showed the absence of cytological atypia, mitoses, and necrosis, and immunohistochemistry ruled out malignancies like liposarcoma (negative for MDM2 and CDK4), vascular tumors (negative for CD31 and HHV8), and other aggressive entities such as MPNST.  
3. **Exclusion of Other Benign Tumors:** Immunohistochemistry helped differentiate the lesion from other benign entities like desmoid tumors (negative for β-catenin), smooth muscle tumors (negative for H-caldesmon and AML), and nerve tumors (negative for S100 and SOX10).  
4. **Confirmation of DBFH:** The combination of CD34 positivity, histologic features (spindle cells, collagen-rich stroma, and encapsulated nature), and lack of markers for other conditions established the diagnosis of deep benign fibrous histiocytoma (DBFH).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Deep Benign Fibrous Histiocytoma (DBFH) in the left thigh. The diagnosis was confirmed by clinical examination, imaging, histopathology, and immunohistochemistry.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 55-year-old with a slowly growing thigh mass. First, the chief complaint is a progressively enlarging mass in the left thigh over six months. No systemic symptoms like fever or weight loss, which might suggest infection or malignancy. Past medical history is unremarkable, which might not contribute much here.

Looking at the physical exam: the mass is firm, non-tender, not fluctuant. No lymphadenopathy. So, maybe a benign tumor, but the size (16 cm) is large. The location is between sartorius and adductor longus muscles, and infiltration near the neurovascular bundle. That's concerning for something that's locally aggressive even if benign.

MRI findings: T1 iso-intense, T2 hyperintense with heterogeneous enhancement. Those features can be seen in various soft tissue tumors. The heterogenous enhancement might suggest areas of necrosis or vascularity. Infiltration into the neurovascular bundle raises the possibility of a locally aggressive tumor, maybe a sarcoma, but the histopathology says benign spindle cells with no atypia or mitoses. Hmm, that's conflicting.

Histopathology shows encapsulated tumor with benign spindle cells, collagen-rich stroma. Immunohistochemistry is positive for CD34 and Desmin. CD34 is seen in various tumors like solitary fibrous tumor, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, or some nerve sheath tumors. Desmin is a muscle marker, but smooth muscle tumors were ruled out (negative for those markers). So, what's positive for both CD34 and Desmin?

Wait, the IHC ruled out desmoid tumor (which is usually positive for beta-catenin but negative for CD34 and Desmin), smooth muscle tumors (SMA, h-caldesmon negative?), nerve tumors (S100 negative?), liposarcoma (MDM2 negative?), vascular tumors (CD31 negative?), solitary fibrous tumor (STAT6 negative?), and striated muscle tumors (myogenin, etc. negative). So the markers that are positive here are CD34 and Desmin. 

What tumors express both CD34 and Desmin? Maybe a myofibroblastic tumor? Or a fibroblastic tumor with myoid differentiation. Wait, desmin is usually in smooth or striated muscle. CD34 is in a variety of mesenchymal cells. Let's think about low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma (LGFMS), but histopathology says no cytological atypia or mitoses. LGFMS typically has bland spindle cells but may have some areas of higher cellularity. But the IHC for LGFMS might show MUC4 positivity, which isn't mentioned here.

Another possibility: benign fibrous histiocytoma? But those are usually smaller and superficial. The size here is 16 cm, which is more in line with a deep soft tissue tumor. 

Angiomyofibroblastoma? Usually occurs in the vulvar region. Not common in thigh. 

Myxoid tumor? But the stroma is collagen-rich, not myxoid.

Wait, the tumor is encapsulated. That's a feature of benign lesions. So maybe a benign peripheral nerve sheath tumor, but S100 is negative. So that's out.

Leiomyoma? But they ruled out smooth muscle tumors with IHC. So desmin positive but SMA negative? That's possible in some cases, but maybe not. Wait, desmin is in smooth muscle, but if SMA is negative, that's odd. Unless it's a myofibroblastic tumor, which can express desmin and SMA. But the IHC here didn't mention SMA. Wait, the IHC negative markers include smooth muscle tumors. So SMA and h-caldesmon would be negative. So desmin positive but SMA negative. That's a bit conflicting. But some tumors can have variable markers.

Another possibility: low-grade myofibroblastic sarcoma. But histopathology here says no cytological atypia or mitoses. So maybe a benign myofibroblastic lesion. 

Wait, the IHC is positive for CD34 and Desmin. CD34 is also positive in solitary fibrous tumor, but they ruled that out with STAT6. So STAT6 negative. So not SFT.

Another thought: the combination of CD34 and Desmin positivity might be seen in a perivascular epithelioid cell tumor (PEComa), but those are usually HMB45 and Melan-A positive. Not mentioned here, and likely excluded.

The tumor is in the thigh, large, encapsulated, with benign histology but infiltrative growth. The key here is the IHC profile. CD34 and Desmin positive, others negative. What's that?

Wait, maybe a cellular angiofibroma? Those are usually in the genital area, but can occur elsewhere. They can have CD34 positivity. Desmin might be variable. 

Alternatively, a benign fibrous tumor with myofibroblastic differentiation. The collagen-rich stroma suggests a fibrotic component. 

Wait, another possibility: a deep fibrous histiocytoma? But they are usually positive for factor XIIIa, CD68, but CD34 is variable. Not sure.

Alternatively, a myxoinflammatory fibroblastic sarcoma? But the histology here is not myxoid. 

Wait, the histopathology mentions sparsely cytoplasmic stroma and collagen-rich bundles. So maybe a collagenous fibroma (also known as desmoplastic fibroblastoma). These are benign, usually in subcutaneous tissue or deep soft tissues. They have spindle cells in a collagenous stroma, no atypia. Immunohistochemistry is usually positive for vimentin, sometimes focal SMA but CD34 can be positive in some cases. However, desmin is typically negative. So this might not fit.

Wait, the patient's tumor is positive for desmin. So that's a key point. What benign spindle cell tumors are desmin positive? Well, smooth muscle tumors, but they were excluded. So maybe a myofibroblastic tumor. Myofibroblasts can express desmin and SMA. But if SMA is negative here (since smooth muscle tumors are excluded), perhaps a desmin-positive myofibroblastic lesion. 

Another angle: the tumor is encapsulated. Encapsulated lesions in soft tissue are often benign. Examples include schwannoma (but S100 negative here), neurofibroma (but S100 usually positive), leiomyoma (excluded), etc. 

Given the IHC profile and the histology, maybe this is a benign myofibroblastic tumor. But the specific type? 

Wait, there's a rare entity called "desmin-positive fibroblastic tumor" or "collagenous fibroma with desmin expression." But I'm not sure. Alternatively, could this be a low-grade myofibroblastic sarcoma, but the histology says no atypia or mitoses, which would be against sarcoma. 

Another thought: the tumor is CD34 and Desmin positive, which might suggest a tumor with both fibroblastic and myoid differentiation. Maybe a myofibroblastoma. Myofibroblastoma typically occurs in the breast, but can occur in other sites. They are composed of spindle cells with features of myofibroblasts. They are positive for desmin and CD34. Yes! That's a possibility. 

Myofibroblastoma is a benign mesenchymal tumor that can occur in soft tissues outside the breast. They are composed of spindle cells with myofibroblastic differentiation. Immunohistochemistry typically shows positivity for desmin and CD34, and they are negative for S100, SMA (variable), and other markers. The histopathology here describes benign spindle cells with no atypia or mitoses, which fits. The encapsulation also aligns with a benign tumor. The location in the thigh is possible. 

So putting it all together: a 16 cm encapsulated tumor with benign histology, positive for CD34 and Desmin, negative for markers of other tumors. The size and location with infiltration might be due to the tumor's size and proximity to structures, but histologically benign. Therefore, the diagnosis is likely a myofibroblastoma. 

Wait, but myofibroblastomas in soft tissue outside the breast are rare, but possible. Another possibility is a solitary fibrous tumor, but STAT6 is negative here. So no. 

Alternatively, maybe a benign peripheral nerve sheath tumor with unusual markers, but S100 is negative. 

Considering all the information, the most fitting diagnosis is myofibroblastoma. The IHC and histology match, and other possibilities are excluded by the negative markers.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a large, slowly growing, painless thigh mass. The absence of systemic symptoms and benign histopathological features (no cytological atypia or mitoses) suggest a benign or low-grade tumor, despite its large size and infiltrative growth on MRI.  
<step 2> MRI findings (T1 iso-intense, T2 hyperintense, heterogenous enhancement) and neurovascular bundle infiltration raise concern for a locally aggressive tumor. However, the encapsulated nature and benign histology argue against high-grade malignancy.  
<step 3> Immunohistochemistry (CD34+/Desmin+) and exclusion of other spindle cell tumors (desmoid, smooth muscle, nerve, liposarcoma, etc.) narrow the differential. CD34 and Desmin co-expression in a benign spindle cell lesion strongly points to a myofibroblastic neoplasm.  
<step 4> The combination of collagen-rich stroma, encapsulation, and absence of atypia/mitoses aligns with a benign myofibroblastic tumor. Myofibroblastoma, though rare in soft tissue, is a plausible diagnosis given the immunoprofile and clinical-pathological correlation.  

### Answer:
Myofibroblastoma

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a large, slowly growing, painless thigh mass. The absence of systemic symptoms and benign histopathological features (no cytological atypia or mitoses) suggest a benign or low-grade tumor, despite its large size and infiltrative growth on MRI.

MRI findings (T1 iso-intense, T2 hyperintense, heterogenous enhancement) and neurovascular bundle infiltration raise concern for a locally aggressive tumor. However, the encapsulated nature and benign histology argue against high-grade malignancy.

Immunohistochemistry (CD34+/Desmin+) and exclusion of other spindle cell tumors (desmoid, smooth muscle, nerve, liposarcoma, etc.) narrow the differential. CD34 and Desmin co-expression in a benign spindle cell lesion strongly points to a myofibroblastic neoplasm.

The combination of collagen-rich stroma, encapsulation, and absence of atypia/mitoses aligns with a benign myofibroblastic tumor. Myofibroblastoma, though rare in soft tissue, is a plausible diagnosis given the immunoprofile and clinical-pathological correlation.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Based on MRI findings of location, size, and heterogenous enhancement, potential diagnoses include schwannomas, leiomyosarcomas, solitary fibrous tumors, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP), and malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST).

Step 2: Histology and immunohistochemistry ruled out malignant tumors by showing absence of cytological atypia, mitoses, and necrosis; negative results for MDM2 and CDK4; and negative for CD31 and HHV8.

Step 3: Further differentiation using immunohistochemistry excluded other benign tumors such as desmoid tumors (negative for β-catenin), smooth muscle tumors (negative for H-caldesmon and AML), and nerve tumors (negative for S100 and SOX10).

Step 4: Diagnosis of Deep Benign Fibrous Histiocytoma (DBFH) confirmed by CD34 positivity, spindle cells, collagen-rich stroma, and encapsulated nature, along with negative markers for other conditions.
Final diagnosis: Deep Benign Fibrous Histiocytoma (DBFH) in the left thigh.
